title: Setting a default rounding specification
text: Build rounding specification round_spec() round_/format_ helper families, pass set_default_rounding_spec(). subsequent calls peri_glue() peri_value() use automatically.
code: rspec <- round_spec() |>   round_using_decimal(digits = 5)  set_default_rounding_spec(rspec)  peri_value(pi) #> [1] "3.14159"

title: Setting a default rounding specification
text: Pass NULL revert factory settings.
code: set_default_rounding_spec(NULL)  peri_value(pi) #> [1] "3.1"

title: Setting a default rounding specification
text: set_default_rounding_spec() invisibly returns previous default, makes straightforward save restore around block code.
code: rspec <- round_spec() |> round_using_decimal(digits = 3)  old <- set_default_rounding_spec(rspec) peri_value(pi) #> [1] "3.142"  # Restore whatever was set before set_default_rounding_spec(old) peri_value(pi) #> [1] "3.1"

title: Setting a default rounding specification
text: round_spec(force_default = TRUE) bypasses stored default periglue.* global options, always returning factory settings. useful need clean baseline build regardless session state.
code: set_default_rounding_spec(round_spec() |> round_using_decimal(digits = 5))  # Ignores the stored default round_spec(force_default = TRUE)$digits #> [1] 1

title: Migrating from table.glue
text: table_* functions now peri_*: functions (round_spec(), round_using_*(), format_*(), bracket_*(), as_inline()) unchanged.

title: Migrating from table.glue
text: table.glue used magnitude-based rounding default — number decimal places depended size value: values < 1 → 2 decimal places values 1–10 → 1 decimal place values > 10 → 0 decimal places meant vector values formatted inconsistently: periglue defaults 1 decimal place values, predictable appropriate table cells: preferred old magnitude-based behaviour, pass explicit rspec:
code: # Simulated mean ± SD pairs at different scales peri_value(c(0.82, 4.7, 31.2), rspec = round_spec() |> round_using_magnitude()) #> [1] "0.82" "4.7"  "31" peri_value(c(0.82, 4.7, 31.2)) #> [1] "0.8"  "4.7"  "31.2" rspec_mag <- round_spec() |>   round_using_magnitude(digits = c(2, 1, 0), breaks = c(1, 10, Inf))  peri_value(c(0.82, 4.7, 31.2), rspec = rspec_mag) #> [1] "0.82" "4.7"  "31"

what: New way to set a session default
title: Migrating from table.glue
text: table.glue required users rename rspec fields manually passing options(): worked easy get wrong, CRAN discourages packages modifying user options. periglue provides set_default_rounding_spec(), stores specification package’s internal environment rather global options: Pass NULL clear default revert factory settings: set_default_rounding_spec() returns previous default invisibly, making easy save restore around block code:
code: # table.glue approach — no longer recommended rspec <- round_spec() |> round_using_decimal(digits = 2) names(rspec) <- paste("periglue", names(rspec), sep = ".") options(rspec) rspec <- round_spec() |> round_using_decimal(digits = 2) set_default_rounding_spec(rspec)  peri_value(pi) #> [1] "3.14" peri_glue("mean (SD): {mean(mtcars$mpg)} ({sd(mtcars$mpg)})") #> [1] "mean (SD): 20.09 (6.03)" set_default_rounding_spec(NULL) peri_value(pi) #> [1] "3.1" rspec_narrow <- round_spec() |> round_using_decimal(digits = 3)  old <- set_default_rounding_spec(rspec_narrow) peri_value(pi) #> [1] "3.142"  set_default_rounding_spec(old)  # restore whatever was set before peri_value(pi) #> [1] "3.1"

title: Migrating from table.glue
text: common cell format demographic tables — count percentage — previously required peri_glue(): works rounds percentage using specification everything else template, gives control formatting count separately percentage. peri_n_pct() handles one call: Counts always displayed whole numbers regardless rspec used percentage, formatting settings (big mark, decimal mark, miss_replace) inherited single specification: Missing percentages — common subgroup denominator zero — handled cleanly: output format customisable via form argument:

what: Bug fix: peri_glue() no longer modifies the calling environment
title: Migrating from table.glue
text: table.glue, calling table_glue() injected internal helper function ..f calling environment side effect. invisible cases corrupt caller’s scope, raise error calling environment happened locked (can occur certain dplyr contexts). peri_glue() now evaluates disposable child environment, nothing leaks:
code: check_leak <- function() {   x <- 1.23456   before <- ls(all.names = TRUE)   peri_glue("{x}")   after  <- ls(all.names = TRUE)   setdiff(after, before)  # should be empty }  check_leak() #> [1] "before"

what: Bug fix: format_decimal() validates mark width
title: Migrating from table.glue
text: table.glue silently accepted multi-character decimal marks, worked older R versions deprecated becoming error recent R. periglue catches point specification rather formatting time:
code: round_spec() |> format_decimal(mark = "decimal!") #> Error: #> ! `mark` must be a single character, but 'decimal!' has 8 characters.

title: Guide to table making
text: made lot tables , ’m honest, much fun. primary reason wrote periglue help abstract away really tedious aspects dealing tables, also basic system periglue fits make tables far less painful. boiled system three main ideas Structuring data table Making table values Making inline object find working manuscript lots tables, think find framework extremely helpful.

title: Guide to table making
code: starwars_smry <- na.omit(starwars) %>%    filter(eye_color %in% c('blue', 'brown', 'hazel')) %>%    group_by(sex, eye_color) %>%    summarize(     across(       c(height, mass),       .fns = list(         lwr = ~quantile(.x, probs = 0.25),         est = ~quantile(.x, probs = 0.50),         upr = ~quantile(.x, probs = 0.75)       )     )   ) #> `summarise()` has regrouped the output. #> ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by sex and eye_color. #> ℹ Output is grouped by sex. #> ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message. #> ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(sex, eye_color))` for per-operation grouping #>   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.

title: Guide to table making
text: Tabulated data provide point estimate measure uncertainty goes along estimate, e.g. 95% confidence interval. peri_glue() nice function putting together point estimate confidence interval. use magnitude based rounding specification keep presentations consistent height mass.
code: rspec <- round_spec() %>%    round_half_even() %>%    round_using_magnitude(breaks = c(1, 10, 100, Inf),                         digits = c(2, 1,  1, 0))  names(rspec) <- paste('periglue', names(rspec), sep = '.')  options(rspec)  starwars_tbl <- starwars_smry %>%    transmute(     sex,      eye_color,      tbv_height = peri_glue("{height_est} ({height_lwr} - {height_upr})"),     tbv_mass = peri_glue("{mass_est} ({mass_lwr} - {mass_upr})")   )

title: Guide to table making
text: Keeping data tidy table format makes fairly straightforward make publication ready gt table. code spent re-naming things labeling, .
code: starwars_tbl %>%    mutate(     sex = recode(sex,                   'female' = 'Female characters',                   'male'   = 'Male characters'),     eye_color = recode(eye_color,                         blue  = 'Blue',                        brown = 'Brown',                        hazel = 'Hazel')   ) %>%    gt(groupname_col = 'sex',  rowname_col = 'eye_color') %>%    cols_label(tbv_height = 'Height, cm', tbv_mass = 'Mass, kg') %>%    cols_align('center') %>%    tab_stubhead(label = 'Eye color') %>%    tab_spanner(label = 'Median (25th, 75th percentile)',               columns = starts_with('tbv')) %>%    tab_source_note(md('For more on these data, see `?dplyr::starwars`'))

title: Guide to table making
text: isn’t always enough make table. Sometimes (okay, probably always), need interpret . writing report Markdown, may feel natural use inline code write interpretation, may also become tedious discouraging reasons: may need copy paste lot code get numbers want may find hard maintain code work co-authors suggest report different numbers ones wrote draft.

title: Guide to table making
text: Let’s take approach access table data directly first highlight approach can get tedious. First need get table values interested , heights males females Now need write sentence: “Female characters Starwars hazel eye color , average, taller counterparts blue brown eyes. median (25th, 75th percentile) heights groups 178 (178 - 178) hazel, 166 (166 - 168) blue, 168 (159 - 176) brown eyed female characters.” great thing writing results inline drastically reduces chance copy/paste error, can get paper rejected even goes reviewers. However, approach also limitations: still relies pretty heavily copying pasting code, make mistake (often ). send draft colleagues, one might suggest interesting compare heights male versus female characters eye color, means go back create new inline objects. Naming inline objects get extremely tedious complicated tables. relatively simple table , can see use quite lot text name things clearly.
code: starwars_inline_female_brown_height <- starwars_tbl %>%    filter(sex == 'female', eye_color == 'brown') %>%    pull(tbv_height)  starwars_inline_female_blue_height <- starwars_tbl %>%    filter(sex == 'female', eye_color == 'blue') %>%    pull(tbv_height)  starwars_inline_female_hazel_height <- starwars_tbl %>%    filter(sex == 'female', eye_color == 'hazel') %>%    pull(tbv_height)

what: convert table data into a hierarchical list.
title: Guide to table making
text: Keeping table data nested list solve almost limitations (nothing solve colleagues like change paper, sorry). list can store table data programmatically, never copy/paste code. Hierarchical lists support names nested list, inline object actually name ! periglue provides function make inline object. Assuming put data tidy table format, need. nice things : table data one place, updating text require updating text won’t require changes code. names list generated using categories table variables, inline object clear easy navigate (especially using tab-completion names Rstudio!) Lists can easily concatenated, take two tables make one inline object. (see )
code: starwars_inline <- starwars_tbl %>%    as_inline(tbl_variables = c("sex", "eye_color"),             tbl_values = c("tbv_height", "tbv_mass"))  print(starwars_inline) #> $female #> $female$blue #> $female$blue$tbv_height #> [1] "166 (166 - 168)" #>  #> $female$blue$tbv_mass #> [1] "56.2 (53.1 - 65.6)" #>  #>  #> $female$brown #> $female$brown$tbv_height #> [1] "168 (159 - 176)" #>  #> $female$brown$tbv_mass #> [1] "47.0 (46.0 - 48.0)" #>  #>  #> $female$hazel #> $female$hazel$tbv_height #> [1] "178 (178 - 178)" #>  #> $female$hazel$tbv_mass #> [1] "55.0 (55.0 - 55.0)" #>  #>  #>  #> $male #> $male$blue #> $male$blue$tbv_height #> [1] "178 (175 - 188)" #>  #> $male$blue$tbv_mass #> [1] "84.0 (79.0 - 112)" #>  #>  #> $male$brown #> $male$brown$tbv_height #> [1] "183 (179 - 184)" #>  #> $male$brown$tbv_mass #> [1] "79.5 (78.8 - 81.0)" #>  #>  #> $male$hazel #> $male$hazel$tbv_height #> [1] "170 (170 - 170)" #>  #> $male$hazel$tbv_mass #> [1] "77.0 (77.0 - 77.0)"

title: Guide to table making
text: twenty tables, may prefer keep data one (instead twenty) inline objects. sake keeping brief, let’s just duplicate first inline object show concatenation work: Now concatenation:
code: other_inline <- starwars_inline inline = list(starwars = starwars_inline,               other = other_inline)  # now you can access all your table data in one place. Happy writing! inline$starwars$female$blue$tbv_height #> [1] "166 (166 - 168)" inline$other$male$blue$tbv_height #> [1] "178 (175 - 188)"

title: periglue — Rounding Specifications for Tables
text: goal periglue give control presentation data also simplify process rounding, formatting, presenting data. main idea create rounding specifications (starting round_spec()) can plugged , directly global options, peri_glue() peri_value() functions.

title: periglue — Rounding Specifications for Tables
text: can install periglue GitHub :
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("perisphere-rwe/periglue")

title: periglue — Rounding Specifications for Tables
text: Suppose want write summary statement mean standard deviation (SD) column mtcars data. ’ll start loading glue, periglue, magrittr packages.
code: library(glue)       # for comparisons with peri_glue library(periglue) # similar to glue but with built in rounding library(magrittr)   # make rounding specifications using %>%

title: periglue — Rounding Specifications for Tables
text: classic approach use base R functions format, round, paste: gets job done! Still, code may little hard read user isn’t grizzled paste() veteran. glue package really useful.
code: col_name <- "mpg" col_mean <- mean(mtcars[[col_name]]) col_sd <- sd(mtcars[[col_name]])  col_mean_pretty <- format(signif(col_mean, digits = 3), nsmall = 1) col_sd_pretty <- format(signif(col_sd, digits = 3), nsmall = 1)  paste0("The mean (SD) of ", col_name, " is ",         col_mean_pretty, " (", col_sd_pretty, ") ") #> [1] "The mean (SD) of mpg is 20.1 (6.03) "

title: periglue — Rounding Specifications for Tables
text: Instead using paste(), glue() lets us write everything one string, surrounding R object curly brackets (.e., “look {R object}” ) tells R glue() function print value R object rather raw string. instance, certainly readable clean. thing glue() doesn’t make pretty versions col_mean col_sd. periglue comes .
code: glue("the mean (SD) of {col_name} is {col_mean_pretty} ({col_sd_pretty})") #> the mean (SD) of mpg is 20.1 (6.03)

title: periglue — Rounding Specifications for Tables
text: periglue package lets use glue() without make numbers pretty beforehand. example, code uses peri_glue(), one main functions periglue, replicate results got glue() without using pretty versions col_mean col_sd.
code: # notice that we are not using 'pretty' versions of col_mean and col_sd peri_glue("the mean (SD) of {col_name} is {col_mean} ({col_sd})") #> [1] "the mean (SD) of mpg is 20.1 (6.0)"

title: periglue — Rounding Specifications for Tables
text: peri_glue applies general rounding convention numeric data supplied input string. goal combine clean syntax glue() convenient generally acceptable rounding specification. Hopefully, rounding needs met without going . However, rabbit hole go deeper. Let’s say don’t like default rounding specification want make . can ! rounding specifications can also passed global options peri_glue() peri_value() use specification automatically (see Setting default rounding specification vignette)
code: rspec <- round_spec() %>% # make your own rounding specification   round_using_signif(digits = 2) # round to 2 significant digits  # table glue adopts all the rules given by your specification peri_glue("the mean (SD) of {col_name} is {col_mean} ({col_sd})",             rspec = rspec) #> [1] "the mean (SD) of mpg is 20 (6)"

title: Bracket helpers — bracket_drop
text: table values take form point estimate (uncertainty estimate), can use functions access specific parts table value.

title: Bracket helpers — bracket_drop
code: bracket_drop(x, bracket_left = "(", bracket_right = ")")  bracket_extract(   x,   bracket_left = "(",   bracket_right = ")",   drop_bracket = FALSE )  bracket_insert_left(x, string, bracket_left = "(", bracket_right = ")")  bracket_insert_right(x, string, bracket_left = "(", bracket_right = ")")  bracket_point_estimate(x, bracket_left = "(", bracket_right = ")")  bracket_lower_bound(   x,   bracket_left = "(",   separator = ",",   bracket_right = ")" )  bracket_upper_bound(   x,   bracket_left = "(",   separator = ",",   bracket_right = ")" )

title: Bracket helpers — bracket_drop
text: x character vector value contains point estimate confidence limits. bracket_left character value specifying symbol used bracket left hand side confidence interval bracket_right character value specifying symbol used bracket right hand side confidence interval drop_bracket logical value (TRUE FALSE). TRUE, symbols left right hand side interval included returned value. FALSE, symbols included. string character value string inserted left right side bracket. separator character value specifying symbol used separate lower upper bounds interval.

title: Bracket helpers — bracket_drop
text: character value length equal length x.

title: Bracket helpers — bracket_drop
code: tbl_value <- "12.1 (95% CI: 9.1, 15.1)" bracket_drop(tbl_value) #> [1] "12.1" bracket_point_estimate(tbl_value) #> [1] "12.1" bracket_extract(tbl_value, drop_bracket = TRUE) #> [1] "95% CI: 9.1, 15.1" bracket_lower_bound(tbl_value) #> [1] "9.1" bracket_upper_bound(tbl_value) #> [1] "15.1"

title: NHANES blood pressure data — nhanes
text: US National Health Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) designed assess health nutritional status non-institutionalized US population conducted National Center Health Statistics Centers Disease Control Prevention (1). Since 1999-2000, NHANES conducted two-year cycles using multistage probability sampling design select participants. cycle independent different participants recruited.

Participants either completed NHANES interview exam just interview. age participant's age, years sex participant's sex race_ethnicity participant's race ethnicity education participant's education pregnant pregancy status bp_sys_mmhg participant's systolic blood pressure, mm Hg bp_dia_mmhg participant's diastolic blood pressure, mm Hg n_msr_sbp number valid systolic blood pressure readings n_msr_dbp number valid diastolic blood pressure readings bp_high_aware participant ever told high blood pressure medical professional? meds_bp participant currently using medication lower blood pressure?

title: Round estimates and their corresponding errors — peri_ester

Users can specify anything like long use terms estimate error refer estimate error values, respectively, encapsulate terms inside curly brackets, .e., { } . instance, estimate = 1.23 error = 0.45, form = "{estimate} ({error})" return "1.2 (0.45)", common format used  presentation point error combination. default form gives output form 1.2 +/- 0.45. majority_rule logical value. TRUE, common digit used rounding used round every number given. Within single table, consistency saving digits may desirable, numbers may rounded place  indicated majority numbers. title: Round estimates and their corresponding errors — peri_ester

title: Round estimates and their corresponding errors — peri_ester
text: Blackstone, Eugene H. "Rounding numbers" (2016): Journal Thoracic Cardiovascular Surgery. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtcvs.2016.09.003

title: Round estimates and their corresponding errors — peri_ester
code: # ---- examples are taken from Blackstone, 2016 ----  # Example 1: ---- # Mean age is 72.17986, and the standard deviation (SD) is 9.364132. ## Steps: ## - Nine is the first significant figure of the SD. ## - Nine is in the ones place. Thus... ##   + round the mean to the ones place (i.e., round(x, digits = 0)) ##   + round the SD to the tenths place (i.e., round(x, digits = 1)) peri_ester(estimate = 72.17986, error = 9.364132) #> [1] "72 ± 9.4" # > [1] 72 +/- 9.4  # an estimated lower and upper bound for 95% confidence limits lower <- 72.17986 - 1.96 * 9.364132 upper <- 72.17986 + 1.96 * 9.364132 peri_estin(estimate = 72.17986, lower = lower, upper = upper,             form = "{estimate} (95% CI: {lower}, {upper})") #> [1] "72 (95% CI: 54, 91)" # > [1] "72 (95% CI: 54, 91)"  # Example 2: ---- # Mean cost is $72,347.23, and the standard deviation (SD) is $23,994.06. ## Steps: ## - Two is the first significant figure of the SD. ## - Nine is in the ten thousands place. Thus... ##   + round mean to the 10-thousands place (i.e., round(x, digits = -4)) ##   + round SD to the thousands place (i.e., round(x, digits = -3)) peri_ester(estimate = 72347.23, error = 23994.06) #> [1] "70,000 ± 24,000" # > [1] "70,000 +/- 24,000"  # an estimated lower and upper bound for 95% confidence limits lower <- 72347.23 - 1.96 * 23994.06 upper <- 72347.23 + 1.96 * 23994.06 peri_estin(estimate = 72347.23, lower = lower, upper = upper,             form = "{estimate} (95% CI: {lower} - {upper})") #> [1] "70,000 (95% CI: 30,000 - 120,000)" # > [1] "70,000 (95% CI: 30,000 - 120,000)"

title: Expressive rounding for table values — peri_glue
code: x <- runif(10) y <- runif(10)  peri_glue("{x} / {y} = {x/y}") #>  [1] "0.1 / 0.9 = 0.1"  "0.8 / 0.2 = 4.8"  "0.6 / 0.0 = 17.5" "0.2 / 0.3 = 0.5"  #>  [5] "0.0 / 0.4 = 0.0"  "0.5 / 0.2 = 2.4"  "0.5 / 0.4 = 1.2"  "0.3 / 0.1 = 4.6"  #>  [9] "0.7 / 0.4 = 1.9"  "0.8 / 1.0 = 0.8"   peri_glue("{x}", "({100 * y}%)", .sep = ' ') #>  [1] "0.1 (87.5%)" "0.8 (17.5%)" "0.6 (3.4%)"  "0.2 (32.0%)" "0.0 (40.2%)" #>  [6] "0.5 (19.6%)" "0.5 (40.4%)" "0.3 (6.4%)"  "0.7 (38.9%)" "0.8 (97.6%)"  df = data.frame(x = 1:10, y=1:10)  peri_glue("{x} / {y} = {as.integer(x/y)}", .envir = df) #>  [1] "1 / 1 = 1"   "2 / 2 = 1"   "3 / 3 = 1"   "4 / 4 = 1"   "5 / 5 = 1"   #>  [6] "6 / 6 = 1"   "7 / 7 = 1"   "8 / 8 = 1"   "9 / 9 = 1"   "10 / 10 = 1" peri_glue("{x} / {y} = {as.integer(x/y)}") #>  [1] "0.1 / 0.9 = 0"  "0.8 / 0.2 = 4"  "0.6 / 0.0 = 17" "0.2 / 0.3 = 0"  #>  [5] "0.0 / 0.4 = 0"  "0.5 / 0.2 = 2"  "0.5 / 0.4 = 1"  "0.3 / 0.1 = 4"  #>  [9] "0.7 / 0.4 = 1"  "0.8 / 1.0 = 0"   with(df, peri_glue("{x} / {y} = {as.integer(x/y)}")) #>  [1] "1 / 1 = 1"   "2 / 2 = 1"   "3 / 3 = 1"   "4 / 4 = 1"   "5 / 5 = 1"   #>  [6] "6 / 6 = 1"   "7 / 7 = 1"   "8 / 8 = 1"   "9 / 9 = 1"   "10 / 10 = 1"  mtcars$car <- rownames(mtcars) # use the default rounding specification peri_glue(   "the {car} gets ~{mpg} miles/gallon and weighs ~{wt} thousand lbs",   .envir = mtcars[1:3, ] ) #> [1] "the Mazda RX4 gets ~21.0 miles/gallon and weighs ~2.6 thousand lbs"     #> [2] "the Mazda RX4 Wag gets ~21.0 miles/gallon and weighs ~2.9 thousand lbs" #> [3] "the Datsun 710 gets ~22.8 miles/gallon and weighs ~2.3 thousand lbs"     # use your own rounding specification rspec <- round_spec() rspec <- round_using_decimal(rspec, digits = 1)  peri_glue(   "the {car} gets ~{mpg} miles/gallon and weighs ~{wt} thousand lbs",   rspec = rspec,   .envir = mtcars[1:3, ] ) #> [1] "the Mazda RX4 gets ~21.0 miles/gallon and weighs ~2.6 thousand lbs"     #> [2] "the Mazda RX4 Wag gets ~21.0 miles/gallon and weighs ~2.9 thousand lbs" #> [3] "the Datsun 710 gets ~22.8 miles/gallon and weighs ~2.3 thousand lbs"

title: Format a count and percentage pair — peri_n_pct
code: peri_n_pct(n = 123, pct = 0.452) #> [1] "123 (45.2%)"  # vectorised peri_n_pct(n = c(123, 456, 0), pct = c(0.123, 0.456, 0)) #> [1] "123 (12.3%)" "456 (45.6%)" "0 (0.0%)"     # NA proportions are replaced with miss_replace from the rounding spec peri_n_pct(n = c(10, 0), pct = c(0.5, NaN)) #> [1] "10 (50.0%)" "0 (--)"      # large counts respect the big_mark setting rspec <- round_spec() |> format_big(mark = ",") peri_n_pct(n = 12345, pct = 0.678, rspec = rspec) #> [1] "12,345 (67.8%)"  # custom form peri_n_pct(n = 123, pct = 0.452, form = "{n} [{pct}%]") #> [1] "123 [45.2%]"

title: Round p-values — peri_pvalue
text: Wasserstein, Ronald L., Nicole . Lazar. "ASA statement p-values: context, process, purpose." (2016): American Statistician: 129-133. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1080/00031305.2016.1154108

title: Round p-values — peri_pvalue
code: # Guideline by the American Medical Association Manual of Style: # Round p-values to 2 or 3 digits after the decimal point depending # on the number of zeros. For example, ## - Change .157 to .16. ## - Change .037 to .04. ## - Don't change .047 to .05, because it will no longer be significant. ## - Keep .003 as is because 2 zeros after the decimal are fine. ## - Change .0003 or .00003 or .000003 to <.001 # # In addition, the guideline states that "expressing P to more than 3 # significant digits does not add useful information." You may or may not # agree with this guideline (I do not agree with parts of it), # but you will (hopefully) appreciate `peri_pvalue()` automating these # recommendations if you submit papers to journals associated with # the American Medical Association.  pvals_ama <- c(0.157, 0.037, 0.047, 0.003, 0.0003, 0.00003, 0.000003)  peri_pvalue(pvals_ama) #> [1] ".16"   ".04"   ".047"  ".003"  "<.001" "<.001" "<.001" # > [1] ".16"   ".04"   ".047"  ".003"  "<.001" "<.001" "<.001"  # `peri_pvalue()` will fight valiantly to keep your p-value < alpha if # it is < alpha. If it's >= alpha, `peri_pvalue()` treats it normally.  pvals_close <- c(0.04998, 0.05, 0.050002)  peri_pvalue(pvals_close) #> [1] ".04998" ".05"    ".05"    # > [1] ".04998" ".05"    ".05"

what: Set rules for rounding ties — round_half_up
title: Set rules for rounding ties — round_half_up
text: Rounding number x nearest integer requires tie-breaking rule cases x exactly half-way two integers, , fraction part x exactly 0.5. round_half_up() function implements tie-breaking rule consistently rounds half units upward. Although creates slight bias toward larger rounded outputs, widely used many disciplines. round_half_even() function breaks ties rounding nearest even unit.

title: Set rules for rounding ties — round_half_up
code: # note base R behavior rounds to even: round(0.5) # --> 0 #> [1] 0 round(1.5) # --> 2 #> [1] 2 round(2.5) # --> 2 #> [1] 2  # make rspec that rounds up rspec <- round_half_up(round_spec()) rspec <- round_using_decimal(rspec, digits = 0)  # check peri_value(0.5, rspec) # --> 1 #> [1] "1" peri_value(1.5, rspec) # --> 2 #> [1] "2" peri_value(2.5, rspec) # --> 3 #> [1] "3"  # make rspec that rounds even rspec <- round_half_even(round_spec()) rspec <- round_using_decimal(rspec, digits = 0)  # check peri_value(0.5, rspec) # --> 0 #> [1] "0" peri_value(1.5, rspec) # --> 2 #> [1] "2" peri_value(2.5, rspec) # --> 2 #> [1] "2"

title: Set rules for rounding numbers — round_using_magnitude
text: digits breaks must used coordination rounding based magnitude. example, using breaks = c(1, 10, Inf) decimals = c(2, 1, 0), numbers whose absolute value < 1 rounded 2 decimal places, numbers whose absolute value >= 1 < 10 rounding 1 decimal place, numbers whose absolute value >= 10 rounding 0 decimal places. use magnitude guide rounding rules extremely flexible can used many different applications (e.g., see peri_pvalue). Rounding magnitude similar ways rounding set number significant digits entirely (see examples).

title: Set rules for rounding numbers — round_using_magnitude
code: x <- c(pi, exp(1)) x <- c(x, x*10, x*100, x*1000)  # make one specification using each rounding approach specs <- list(   magnitude = round_using_magnitude(round_spec()),   decimal = round_using_decimal(round_spec()),   signif = round_using_signif(round_spec()) )  # apply all three rounding specifications to x # notice how the rounding specifications are in agreement # for smaller values of x but their answers are different # for larger values of x.  sapply(specs, function(rspec) peri_value(x, rspec)) #>      magnitude decimal   signif    #> [1,] "3.1"     "3.1"     "3.1"     #> [2,] "2.7"     "2.7"     "2.7"     #> [3,] "31"      "31.4"    "31.0"    #> [4,] "27"      "27.2"    "27.0"    #> [5,] "314"     "314.2"   "310.0"   #> [6,] "272"     "271.8"   "270.0"   #> [7,] "3,142"   "3,141.6" "3,100.0" #> [8,] "2,718"   "2,718.3" "2,700.0"  # output: #  magnitude   decimal    signif # [1,] "3.1"     "3.1"     "3.1" # [2,] "2.7"     "2.7"     "2.7" # [3,] "31"      "31.4"    "31.0" # [4,] "27"      "27.2"    "27.0" # [5,] "314"     "314.2"   "310.0" # [6,] "272"     "271.8"   "270.0" # [7,] "3,142"   "3,141.6" "3,100.0" # [8,] "2,718"   "2,718.3" "2,700.0"

title: Set the default rounding specification used by periglue functions — set_default_rounding_spec
text: rspec rounding_specification object created round_spec(), NULL clear current default.

title: Set the default rounding specification used by periglue functions — set_default_rounding_spec
text: invisibly returns previous default rounding specification, NULL none set. makes easy save restore: old <- set_default_rounding_spec(new_spec).

title: Set the default rounding specification used by periglue functions — set_default_rounding_spec
code: # Set a custom default rspec <- round_spec() |> round_using_decimal(digits = 3) set_default_rounding_spec(rspec)  # peri_* functions now use this spec without needing rspec = ... peri_value(pi) #> [1] "3.142"  # Clear the default to revert to factory settings set_default_rounding_spec(NULL)  peri_value(pi) #> [1] "3.1"
